title: Charting causal set configuration space with graph observables
authors: Astrid Eichhorn, Harald Mack, Kim Tuyen Le, Fabian Wagner
abstract: The configuration space of causal sets is vast. It is a critical goal to map out this space. Here, we take a practical step towards this goal. We investigate nine classes of causal sets, most of them not studied before. These include manifoldlike causal sets with inhomogeneous Ricci curvature, both topologically trivial and nontrivial. We also study classes of non-manifoldlike causal sets, including lattices, layered orders as well as Lorentzian quasicrystals. Finally, we study classes of causal sets that are not manifoldlike, but are expected to become manifoldlike under a suitable coarse-graining process. We use this broad range of distinct classes of causal sets as a testbed for observables. Rather than focusing on continuum-geometry inspired observables, such as curvature invariants, which often exhibit large fluctuations and are computationally very expensive, we focus on graph observables, including some observables that constitute subgraph statistics and some that are global. We find that three observables, namely the link degree distribution, the eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian of the symmetrized Hasse diagram and the recently proposed abundance of causal intervals, can distinguish between the distinct classes of causal sets. This is made possible by the small fluctuations that these observables have in most classes.
summary: This paper proposes a method to characterize the configuration space of causal sets using graph-theoretic observables rather than expensive continuum-geometry invariants. It demonstrates that simple structural properties like link degree and Laplacian eigenvalues can effectively differentiate between different spacetime-like and non-manifoldlike discrete structures.
main_problem: The goal is to map the vast configuration space of causal sets and identify efficient, label-invariant observables that can distinguish between manifoldlike and non-manifoldlike structures.
main_result: Three graph observables—link degree distribution, eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian, and the abundance of causal intervals—are found to be highly effective at distinguishing between nine different classes of causal sets.
method: The authors use numerical simulations of various causal set classes (including Poisson sprinklings, lattices, and quasicrystals) and apply graph-theoretic analysis and statistical convergence tests.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Causal Set Theory, investigating various discrete spacetime models including manifoldlike sets (with varying curvature and topology), non-manifoldlike sets (lattices, layered orders, and Kleitman-Rothschild orders), and Lorentzian quasicrystals.
key_observables: Link degree distribution, eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian of the symmetrized Hasse diagram, abundance of causal intervals (Alexandrov intervals), and height profiles.
important_parameters: Causal set size (n), spacetime dimensionality (d), boundary type (box vs. diamond), conformal factor (curvature), and topological parameters (genus, number of branches).
main_assumptions: The study is primarily restricted to conformally flat manifolds (Weyl tensor = 0) and focuses on 2D and 3D dimensions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate topological changes via cuts and handles, geometric parameters of lattices, and the sensitivity of various graph observables to dimensionality, size, and boundary conditions.
paper_structure: The paper defines various classes of causal sets, introduces a framework for generating them via Poisson sprinkling and other methods, presents a suite of graph-based observables, and evaluates their ability to distinguish between the constructed classes through statistical analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. We provide an example of a box in 1+1 dimensional spacetime (on which a nontrivial conformal factor can determine the sprinkling density), in which we insert incisions starting from the future spacelike boundary. They result in changes of the spatial topology as a function of time, such that the topology differs between the hypersurfaces Σ1 through Σ4. Topology in this context refers to whether the spatial hypersurfaces are simply connected or whether they consist of (a varying number of) multiple disconnected pieces.
FIG. 2. We change the spacetime topology by excising cuts that do not meet the boundary of the spacetime region. This is equivalent to adding handles to a spacetime.
FIG. 3. Left: illustration of two characteristic segments of a grid with relative length ρ = ∥⃗b∥/∥⃗a∥and the enclosed angle θ. Right: Overall rotation angle with respect to x-axis γ.

FIG. 4. Left: Sketch of projection of hypercubic lattice in Cartesian coordinates in M3,1 (one spatial dimension suppressed) to quasicrystal in M1,1. Right: Embedding of example quasicrystal in M1,1 with causal-diamond boundary.

FIG. 5. Left: Distribution of average connectivities in two-dimensional topologically trivial spacetimes, empirically obtained from 104 causets of 2048 elements. This is the sampling probability used to generate random partial orders with fixed average connectivity. Right: Distribution over resulting Myrheim-Meyer dimension compared with that of topologically trivial spacetimes obtained from ensembles of 104 causets of 2048 elements.
